sentences.text: Not too much more.
I know that it affected the fact that my father lost his export license.
So that part of the business was gone.
We had land near Kisvarda.
That was taken away.
And we just couldn't go to higher schools.
I-- otherwise I don't remember.
Well, my mother thought, or I thought, I'd better learn how to sew.
I better learn something.
A trade, which I did.
I learned, well, mainly everything about it, like cutting and sewing.
And-- and I always designed everything for my dolls.
I mean, they wore the same clothes as my mother did.
I had access to beautiful fabrics, and I loved to do that.
Well, I don't remember.
I guess so.
But not all of them.
You know, some--
What did he do, Fischel?
I-- I guess I don't know.
No, I don't remember.
It must have been just the end of the world, you know, because they started right away, the Nyilas party in Hungary, which really brought them in.
The Nyilas was the Hungarian fascist party.
And they immediately started to speak against the Jews.
And we're going to get the Jews.
And the Jews are no good.
On the radio.
I heard this on the radio.
But you know, when you're a child, it doesn't sink in.
You're still hopeful.
You don't believe that that is real.
You know, You don't want to believe that tragedy is going to happen to us.
We all felt very scared.
And as I tell you, I was too young to believe it.
And I had to eventually.
[CHUCKLES]
Because pretty soon they got us together.
They just let us stay home for Passover, which my mother bought 300 eggs.
Because she said, the day they took us away to the ghetto, she took 50 eggs.
And I said, I am making a big omelette or something.
This may be the last time I'm feeding my family.
She knew it.
Yes.
She knew it.
She was very alert.
I don't think anybody came.
They just told us what to do.
Take, I don't know, a few belongings, and no dishes, nothing, and that we had to go to one room was assigned to us on [HUNGARIAN].
And we just were already in a daze.
And we all went.
I think the Hungarian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gendarme was very much involved with this.
And the Germans sent them first.
I think that maybe.
And-- but we knew.
They came to your house.
Hungarian gendarme.
And you had to get yourself together, just how many pants you can take along, and--
The gendarme was the Hungarian police, which was, of course, part of that whole fascistic country.
Well, but they were anti-Semites.
And they were glad to do this.
Not really.
Not really.
Even there I have a lot of lost memory.
But I remember something funny that my brother's friend says.
You know, I'm in love with your little sister, Regina.
And I said to him, no way.
I don't care for him.
[CHUCKLES] That human beings want to hold onto life, and some kind of normalcy, except in Auschwitz.
Right.
One street.
One room, seven people we were in.
Because there were Hungarian-- Kisvarda and vicinity, and I think there were 3,000.
No, no.
I don't think so.
Well, we didn't go out much, no.
We were afraid.
No.
No.
Maybe my brother did because he wanted to be able to get some food or something.
He became the father at his tender age.
And I think he went out.
But we didn't go out.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
Yeah, but going on 10, as I told you, because I was a very protected child.
Orthodox, very fine Jewish family, yes.
Very protected.
No, not at all.
No.
Actually--
No, I have conception of that.
Must have been--
But I don't remember.
Well, she was just trying to hold us together.
And she didn't show her fear because she loved us all, and she had to be strong.
She said, while I have my children together, it's all right.
Yes, yes.
Yeah, it lasted till May 28, when deportation started.
Well, they didn't tell us Auschwitz.
They didn't tell us anything.
Just, you're going to get on this train, which was a cattle train.
Yeah, I remember that day.
They had everybody's name, and they had everybody's family.
And they had us line up with almost-- not even a suitcase.
Hungarians, yeah.
I think we didn't get to the Germans till we got to the train.
And we just had very little things.
And we had to carry it on our back.
And we went, all the 3,000 people kind of marched through town.
And the Hungarian peasants and everybody was lining the streets and laughing and saying things against the Jews.
And by that time, we couldn't understand what we were doing.
In a matter of less than two months, we became nonpeople.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And--
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes I do.
Nobody.
Nothing-- nothing-- nothing-- nothing.
Enough.
Yes.
Yes, and they yell and scream, you know.
No dogs-- I-- very few dogs.
It was nothing like the German army, no.
German SS, that is.
But it was bad, very bad.
During the day.
A beautiful day.
May is a beautiful month.
And--
I am sure they did, you know, just antisemitic remarks.
No.
I don't remember.
Right.
Laughing maybe, too.
Yes.
You know, like-- I don't know what they said.
Yes.
I think to the railroad station.
And there already were the Nazis, the SS--
--With the dogs, working with the Hungarians, the police.
And they pushed us on this horrendous-- I don't know how many people in one car.
No, no.
It was a-- a cattle car.
Yeah, there would be no seats.
I think we had to sit on the floor.
And we were all-- you know, all together with my mother.
You know.
And she just looked at us with those beautiful, sad eyes and repeating again, as long as I have my children, everything is all right.
But it wasn't.
Well, you couldn't breathe.
It took two days, or two nights.
We didn't know where we were going.
But we were shipped to Auschwitz.
Well, I could tell you this one story that my brother got off, and somehow he wanted to escape.
No.
No he didn't.
And he heard my mother say, I have an only son, and he has left me.
And he came back.
He heard my mother say that.
And he couldn't leave my mother.
There was such love between-- you know, my only boy, you know, my mother's only son.
I think she did.
I think because she read-- we used to have Polish escapees in our house.
And my mother took part in everything that had-- that the war brought to us.
You know?
And I think she knew it.
But she wouldn't.
Yeah.
And then my mother took them to Budapest.
And-- and we-- it was an unbelievable chance she took.
Polish Jews.
Somewhere, or ghettos.
I don't know.
And my mother took them up to Budapest.
There was some organization, you know.
Well, no.
More with Budapest, more important people, you know, who could-- there were very few people who could do-- escape from Poland.
But there were a few.
And it was your life was in danger if you took them in.
And they couldn't speak Hungarian.
And anyway, my mother felt that she has to do good for everybody that comes to her.
She was that kind of a self-sacrificing human being.
She felt for every human being.
Her children came first, but after that, humanity.
She was a real humanitarian.
And thank God we have learned this from her, and even our children, and I think our grandchildren, know this, that we come from a good family where we care for every human being.
Well, we hear all this screaming.
It sounded like animals screaming, and German.
The train stopped.
And they pushed us out fast.
Everything was fast.
And lined us up, I guess five, because that was their magic number.
You know, five in every line, in every row, I think.
Or maybe-- maybe we were more as we came off the train.
And all of a sudden, we see this gorgeous German soldier, officer, with big German shepherds, you know, and yelling and screaming.
And we thought that we arrived in an insane asylum.
It had no reality factor.
And I think that I remember that my mind went also like berserk.
This is not real.
And I went into another world, immediately into shock.
Yes, his name-- well, later on, not then, but a couple of days later we found out that he was Mengele.
And he was the most important man there.
And he had to say life or death.
His right arm meant life, his left was death.
We didn't know this until we arrived to the cells.
You know, but-- first he took out my older sister Tipi, Chicha, and Isabelle to the right.
Right.
[NON-ENGLISH].
You know, fast, fast, fast.
And then the line-- I went with my mother, my little sister.
And I guess my brother was with men, so I don't remember what happened to him.
But they-- they turned us around when I was with my mother and my little sister.
And they turned us around, and they said--
The Germans.
Mengele, because he didn't take out enough people to the right hand side, which we only knew retroactively, you know, that--
Again, yeah.
And he pulled me out.
And I-- to the right hand side.
Left, with my mother.
And I think that I was put out there, even though I was young, I was busty, and I looked-- the Hungarian women looked healthy.
And even I looked healthy.
And then when I saw him pull me out, I looked at my mother and my little sister.
And I said to Mengele, [GERMAN].
My little sister.
Let her go anyway, I said.
Let her go with my mother.
I didn't know they're going to their death.
But my mother knew it.
I'm sure of that.
And that hurts.
And I will never get over that, never in my life.
To walk into your death.
Not many people knew that, but my mother knew it from the Polish Jews.
And she was a very alert woman who knew politics and everything.
Yeah.
Yeah.
But I still feel that I had something-- I had said the wrong thing.
I didn't know then.
I thought I'm doing my little sister a favor.
Let her go with my mother, she is so attached to her.
But then, ever since then, I feel that I killed her.
Because I made her go with my mother.
You're so right, but I can't say that.
I-- because I said that she is only 13, I blame myself, 50 years now, that I killed my little sister.
One's mind, when it goes berserk, how can you tell that person how I should feel?
And I remained with this feeling up until today.
And I told my doctor that it doesn't look likely that I am going to get over this.
And it's been-- actually crippled me all these 50 years.
There were other things too, but this is the major theme of my illness.
Well, I became a depressive, and seriously depressed.
No, no, no.
No, no, no.
I was-- you know, kind of very active child, you know.
Well, then they were screaming and yelling.
They took us into this place where they undressed us, and they put a rag on us, and they take our hair off.
They-- they-- they shaved our head.
And we looked at each other, and we didn't recognize each other.
There may have been Polish or Russian or some kind of people who already were there in Auschwitz--
--Prisoners who did that.
But our minds were totally gone.
And we said this is an insane asylum.
And we hardly recognized each other.
And you didn't know where you were.
And you couldn't think that this is, really in a way, your death, the end of your life.
Even though I'm alive today, I think my life somehow ended at that moment, because I've never been normal since then.
I had been so affected.
Depressed, going from one doctor to another for at least 40 years or 42 years, yes.
And it's my--
I don't remember.
And they put us in this large, what they call Lager C.
And then they had these big rooms where there were only like wooden boxes.
And each wooden box had to hold 12 of us, like we were into each other like-- not animals, because animals have some--
--Some way to move.
We didn't.
We just were fitted kind of into each other, 12 people in every one of those.
And I--
--think they had a thousand people in every lager.
And there were thousands of-- and there were I don't know how many in a lager.
We remained together.
That was definitely so because we had to.
We made an agreement that we're going to try to stay together.
Till the bitter end we did stay together.
All that time.
And we got there, these block altestes, who worked with the Germans, screamed, unheard of voices, you know, like very deep and very loud, if you're not going to do this or that, you going to go up.
Do you see that smoke?
That's where your mother is.
And you're going to go up in that smoke.
How could you believe that?
You-- I mean, you're coming from-- not just two months ago, I was in my home.
And you-- you just don't know who you are, don't know where you are.
And you know, a hopelessness and some kind of almost madness.
Your mind becomes, you know, like a veil over your mind, you know?
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and we remained there for quite a few months.
Went through selections, which was the biggest farce.
Well, you had to get up early  in the morning and line up in fives.
Probably 6 o'clock.
I don't know.
And each block or each you know, cell or something, had a thousand people in it.
And you had to line up in five.
Now, we were five sisters.
Rows of five, up onto a thousand.
And you stood there for hours, maybe-- maybe five, six hours or more.
I don't remember.
And--
Did nothing.
And the sun and the horrible climate burning you down.
And then a German woman, Grese, came with a dog for selection.
A German Shepherd.
Grese, of course, was murdered, you know, hung after the war.
Grese was the woman equivalent of Dr.
Mengele.
You know, she was a big shot.
That's the way she was called.
We don't know any other name, Grese.
In my camp, only women.
And she looks through every row and counts every people, that nobody is missing.
Well, where could we go?
This was called selection.
And if somebody didn't look so good, she pulled them out.
And I guess they got them together from the whole camp, and they took them to the crematorium.
That we learned later on.
Healthy or strong.
And my sisters were terribly worried about me because I was so little.
And they used to make my cheeks a little redder.
And--
They went like this, like this.
You know, and they kind of pushed me up.
I should look taller.
You know?
And they were terribly worried about me.
That's why I think my little sister would have never survived, because I hardly survived, and she was five years younger than I was.
And I still feel that that I killed her.
Counted you, yeah.
Now, from then on, we figured it out that we should hide from every zahlappell, the five of us.
And we somehow managed to do this for months.
I mean, when we heard that they're coming, we-- went-- it was a very big place.
We'd go into the washroom, or we'd hide.
We were just running like maniacs because we didn't want to see one of us taken away or two of us.
My sister Chicha--
Berta.
Actually, Isabelle got the job in a place called [GERMAN]--
In Auschwitz, in our block, where they selected all the clothes that we brought in.
And because of that, she got one piece of bread-- which wasn't bread, I think.
It was sawdust.
But that was more than not having it.
And then she got some kind of little typhoid, and we had to take care of her.
And she pushed Chicha into her job.
And Chicha came home one night with a knife.
And that was like having everything.
You didn't have to break your bread.
You could, for five people, cut very thin pieces and-- and, you know, divide it equally.
There was one soup, once a day, maybe in the afternoon.
And it was like-- how do you say, a big sty, what they give for pigs?
You know?
Slop.
It looked like slop.
I think-- I don't know whether they were prisoners, or Germans came with a truck or something.
They were always screaming about everything, you know?
Like we should stay here, we should stay there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well I didn't think that I would remember all this much.
So you have helped me to remember all this.
I don't want to remember it.
It still hurts.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every moment was a fear from the crematorium.
Not-- yeah, they first gassed them.
Sometimes I heard they didn't even die.
Very good.
OK.
